EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains produced flash flooding in Pine Bluffs, WY.  Market Street was closed for a time with 3.10 inches of rain reported.
A weak storm produced a microburst near Burns, Wy.
A severe storm produced some hail over far eastern Platte county.
Slow moving severe thunderstorms produced large hail over parts of Albany and Laramie counties of extreme southeast Wyoming.
Slow moving severe thunderstorms produced large hail over parts of Albany and Laramie counties of extreme southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving severe thunderstorm produced hail from 1 to 1.75 inches in diameter along a track from south of Cheyenne to Hillsdale, Wyoming, about 16 miles east of Cheyenne.  Some windows and skylights were broken in Hillsdale, WY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving severe thunderstorms produced large hail over parts of Albany and Laramie counties of extreme southeast Wyoming.
Heavy rain produced minor flooding on U.S. highway 85 around mile marker 26.
High winds returned to southeast Wyoming with the strongest winds reported over parts of Carbon, Platte and Laramie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 60 to 70 mph with a peak gust of 80 mph on Interstate 25 at the Bordeaux exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm drifted along the Wyoming/Nebraska border and produced some large hail near Albin, WY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly smaller, and rather soft hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of severe thunderstorms produced hail over parts of central Laramie county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many reports of hail from 1/2 to 1 inch in diameter in and around Cheyenne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced some hail and flash flooding over  southern Platte and western Laramie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported on Interstate 25 near mile marker 58.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced some hail and flash flooding over  southern Platte and western Laramie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County road 109 washed out in 2 places with up to a foot of water across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced very heavy rains over parts of Cheyenne which resulted in some flash flooding of streets and drainages.
Slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rain which produced some flash flooding in and near Carpenter, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in Carpenter and along parts of Cottonwood Creek with some county roads flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded a portion of U.S. highway 30/287 near Rock River, WY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water up to 8 inches deep flowing across highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through central Wyoming bringing deep, moist northerly upslope flow to favored locations.  Six to ten inches of snow fell from Lander to Sweetwater Station.  Amounts of 12 to 14 inches were observed along the east slopes of the Wind River Mountains.  Sustained north wind of 20 to 30 mph with occasional higher gusts made for dangerous traveling conditions across central Wyoming.

Amounts of 12 to 14 inches were observed along the east slopes of the Wind River Mountains.  Sustained north wind of 20 to 30 mph with occasional higher gusts made for dangerous traveling conditions across central Wyoming.
Strong westerly flow produced high wind over Chief Joseph Pass where sustained wind was measured between 50 and 70 mph along State Highway 296.

A tightening pressure gradient ahead of an approaching cold front produced high wind across much of Natrona County.  A peak gust of 68 mph was recorded in Casper.  An automated station in western Natrona County measured gusts of 71 and 73 mph.

A tightening pressure gradient ahead of an approaching cold front produced high wind across much of Natrona County.  A peak gust of 68 mph was recorded in Casper.  An automated station in western Natrona County measured gusts of 71 and 73 mph.

A Pacific storm system and associated cold front caused heavy snow and considerable wind across western Wyoming on Christmas Eve.  Snowfall of around six inches was reported in the Star Valley with slightly more than one foot in the nearby mountains.  As the storm moved east of the Continental Divide it brought favorable northerly upslope flow to the Lander Foothills.  A quick six to eight inches of snow was recorded by early Christmas morning in and near Lander.

A favorable jet stream pattern and a tightening pressure gradient combined to create gusty west-northwest wind across portions of central Wyoming.

A Jackson, Wyoming resident was buried under five feet of snow in an avalanche near Bondurant while snowmobiling on Sunday, December 2.  The slide occurred in the Cliff Creek area and ran downslope for about 200 yards.  The man was rescued by fellow riders when they were able to locate his avalanche beacon.

A strong surface pressure gradient and very strong mountain top wind combined to create hurricane force wind near Clark.  Lesser speeds were measured in adjacent and nearby mountain areas.  NWS personnel measured frequent gusts of 75 to 95 mph near Clark with a peak gust measured at 110 mph.  Sustained wind on Chief Joseph Pass hovered between 40 and 60 mph, while Beaver Rim had sustained speeds topping 40 mph.

Strong westerly flow off the Pacific Ocean brought heavy snow and gusty west to southwest wind to Teton County.  Snowfall of six to eight inches was common around Moose and other locations in central Grand Teton National Park.  Snowfall on the Teton Range totaled one foot.

Southwest flow ahead of an approaching Pacific storm system produced high wind over central Wyoming in response to a strengthening pressure gradient.
Heavy snowfall recorded over the Snowy/Sierra Madre ranges of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated snowfall amounts from 12 to 17 inches recorded at SnoTel sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southwest flow combined with northerly upslope in the wake of a weak cold front helped to generate a period of heavy snow across northeast Johnson County.  Snowfall of up to six inches was recorded in Buffalo.

One skier was critically injured in Grand Teton National Park when an avalanche swept him 2,000 feet down the TNT Couloir in Granite Canyon.  The skier triggered the 3-foot deep avalanche when he attempted to ski down the couloir.

Deep low pressure in the Northern Plains and high pressure in the Great Basin combined to create a strong pressure gradient across Wyoming. Sustained wind of 40 to 50 mph with gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common. A peak peak gust of 90 mph was recorded on Chief Joseph Pass in Park County. Other notable peak gusts included 71 mph at Buffalo and 66 mph at both Riverton Regional Airport and Beaver Rim.

Other notable peak gusts included 71 mph at Buffalo and 66 mph at both Riverton Regional Airport and Beaver Rim.

An avalanche was triggered by a skier near a cliff in Rock Springs Bowl just south of the Jackson Hole Mountain Resort.  The skier jumped over the cliff to avoid the avalanche, but he suffered massive internal injuries upon impact and was killed.  Two companions were swept over the cliff, landing on their backs, and were unhurt.

An arctic cold front roared across central Wyoming during the early morning hours.  Snowfall of two to four inches fell in Fremont County, while heavier snow of 6 to 12 inches occured in Natrona County.  Sustained north-northeast wind of 20 to 25 mph was common.  Higher gusts between 30 and 40 mph created three to four foot snowdrifts near Lysite.

A strengthening pressure gradient produced strong southwest wind across central Wyoming.  Peak winds occurred during the early morning with a gust to 61 mph near Casper.  Sustained wind of 40 to 45 mph and gusts near 70 mph occurred in the Green and Rattlesnake Mountains.

Strong westerly flow off the Pacific brought moisture and strong winds to the mountains of western Wyoming.  Snow accumulations of 8 to 16 inches were widespread with an estimated peak amount of 17 inches at Lewis Lake Divide.

A slow-moving low pressure system produced widespread heavy snow and gusty wind crippling much of central Wyoming.  Strong southerly pre-frontal wind gusts of around 60 mph were common ahead of the storm.  Blizzard conditions were reported from Buffalo south to Casper as strong northeast upslope flow developed as the storm progressed east.  Frequent reports of sustained wind of 35 mph with gusts to around 50 mph were received.  The peak wind gust at Buffalo, one of the hardest hit areas, was 64 mph.|Impressive snowfall was received in conjunction with the prolonged period of upslope flow.  Snowfall amounts of three to five feet were common in the Bighorn and Wind River Mountains, and on Casper Mountain.  The peak snow total was estimated to be six feet at the Hobbs Park SNOTEL in the Wind River Mountains.  Most lower elevation locations reported snowfall of 8 to 20 inches.  However, the favored upslope location of Lander received around 30 inches of snow.|The combination of the wind and snow caused many roads to be closed.  By Thursday morning most routes out of Casper and Buffalo were closed with drifts of several feet being reported in some locations.  A multi-vehicle crash on Interstate 90 north of Buffalo closed that road early Wednesday.  The crash involved a bus carrying 39 students, seven semi-tractor trailers, and 11 other vehicles.  Approximately 1,000 gallons of diesel fuel spilled and four injuries occurred with this incident.|Early estimates show that upwards of 1,000 head of livestock were probably lost during the storm and at one time more than 2,000 homes were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious moisture was brought north into Wyoming in strong monsoonal flow.  Low-level upslope flow from the north aided the development of showers and thunderstorms.  Atmospheric moisture values were around 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a mudslide across East Entrance Road near Sylvan Pass.  The slide varied in depth from one to five feet, was about 50 feet long, and covered both lanes of the road.  The road remained closed for about 14 hours until crews could open a single lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture in association with the Southwest Monsoon streamed north into western Wyoming.  Heavy rainfall in the foothills of the Teton Range west of Jackson triggered a debris flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain triggered a debris flow in Jensen Canyon shortly before 3:00 p.m.  Eyewitness reports indicated that entire trees, boulders, and mud were moved by the debris flow which was three to eight feet deep.  The flow pushed an automobile for 20 to 30 feet before flipping it onto its side.  A small outbuilding near one residence was also damaged.  A geologist measured a mud line 40 feet up the sides of the canyon upstream from the canyon mouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in southerly flow ahead of a vigorous late spring storm system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through central Wyoming during the evening of Saturday, October 20 bringing colder air and upslope flow to Casper Mountain and nearby locations.  Snowfall generally ranged from 14 to 16 inches on the top of Casper Mountain with over one inch of liquid precipitation commonly reported.  Lesser amounts of 6 to 8 inches were reported along the mountain's west and north slopes.
Banded snow showers moved slowly south across Star Valley during the evening hours of Saturday, October 20.  The heaviest snow of six to nine inches fell around Thayne and Star Valley Ranch.

A powerful Spring upper level low slowly moved across Central  and Northern Wyoming during a 48 hour period . As a result, a heavy, prolonged precipitation event occurred. The precipitation started as rain but quickly changed over to snow. The snow became heavy at times with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour. In addition to the heavy snow, very strong northwest winds of 25 to 40 mph created blizzard conditions across portions of Northern Wyoming. Zero to near zero visibilities were common as a result of the combined heavy snow and strong winds. |The following are the many reports that were received from this major Spring blizzard: 30 inches in Sheridan(up to 36 inches around town), 20 inches with 5 foot drifts 25E Sheridan, 36 inches 16S Sheridan(10 foot drifts in Banner), 26 inches 8SSW Sheridan(5 foot drifts and 30 feet wide in Big Horn Wyoming), 34 inches 15SSE Sheridan, 42 inches 12SSW Dayton(Sucker Creek Snotel two-day total), 48 inches 21S Sheridan(Dome Lake Snotel two-day total), 39 inches 27S Sheridan(Cloud Peak Reservoir Snotel two-day total), 34 inches 8WSW Dayton(Tie Creek Snotel two-day total), 41 inches 14WSW Dayton(Burgess Junction Snotel two-day total), 51 inches 19SW Sheridan(Big Goose Snotel two-day total), 20 inches with 6 to 7 foot drifts 5SW Clearmont(two-day total) and 16 inches with 6 to 8 foot drifts and communications lost 15NNE Clearmont.

A Pacific cold front moved through North Central Wyoming. Close behind this front, an arctic front swept across the forecast area bringing bitterly cold temperatures. Snow developed across the area, with the most significant accumulations over the Big Horn Mountains and adjacent foothills, especially on north facing slopes. In addition to the snow, gusty winds combined with very cold temperatures created dangerous wind chills of 10 to 25 degrees below zero at times. The following snow totals were received: 9 inches 18SE Burgess Junction(Dome Lake Snotel), 8 inches in Burgess Junction and Sheridan and 10 inches 2W Sheridan.

NWS personnel measured frequent gusts of 75 to 95 mph near Clark with a peak gust measured at 110 mph. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Evanston, Wyoming received 6 inches of snow in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture combined with strong low pressure and upslope flow to create widespread snow and wind across western and central Wyoming.  Northeast wind of 20 to 30 mph with occasional higher gusts combined to create near-blizzard conditions at times.  Most high school sporting events were cancelled and numerous car accidents were attributed to the treacherous road conditions.||Snowfall of six to twelve inches fell in the favored upslope areas in Fremont and Natrona counties.  Similar amounts of six to ten inches were observed in southern Lincoln and western Sweetwater counties.  Snowfall along and south of the I-80 corridor through Sweetwater County only ranged from two to six inches; however, the wind combined with this snowfall to close roads across the area.  Total storm snowfall just topping one foot was recorded in the eastern Wind River Mountains.

Persistent moist westerly flow from the Pacific Ocean created a prolonged period of snow and gusty wind across western Wyoming.  Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph were common along with heavy snow, which combined to cause white-out conditions at times.  Snowfall totals in the Jackson and Star valleys generally ranged from six to eleven inches.  Amounts of one to two feet of snow fell in the Teton, Gros Ventre, Wyoming, and Salt River mountain ranges.

A strong cold front pushed quickly south across Johnson County during the early morning hours.  Snow developed behind the front, temperatures plunged to below zero, and gusty north-northwest wind of 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 44 to 49 mph were common.  Visibility was frequently one-quarter mile or less between 7:30 a.m. and 4:00 p.m.  Snow accumulations averaged around two inches.  Interstate 90 north of Buffalo was closed due to the strong winds and snow.

Strong, warm winds aloft combined with very cold air in the mountains created favorable conditions for strong winds in the Sheridan area. The following wind reports were received: 45 mph sustained winds and a 74 mph wind gust in Sheridan.

A strong cold front and upper level disturbance moved across North Central Wyoming during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 13th. As a result, severe thunderstorms producing strong gusty winds moved across Sheridan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds blew over parts of southeast Wyoming, with the strongest winds occurring over the favored area of southern Platte county where tractor trailer trucks were blown over.  A Wyoming highway department sensor recorded a peak gust of 101 mph on Interstate 25 near the Bordeaux exit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds from 40 to 50 mph occurred along Interstate 25 between Chugwater and Wheatland, WY., with a gust of 101 mph recorded near the Bordeaux exit.  Between 10 and 11:15 AM there were 11 tractor-trailer trucks reported blown over resulting in 4 minor injuries, with windows blown out of some vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper level disturbance moved south out of Canada resulting in very strong winds across North Central Wyoming. The following two wind reports were received: 66 mph gust 7SW Sheridan and |a 78 mph gust in Sheridan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to southwest flow created strong surface wind in favored areas of northwest and central Wyoming.

Favorable conditions led to a mountain wave event in some locations across northwest Wyoming. Enhanced northwest flow led to very high wind channeling down the Wind River Valley.  Mountain locations experienced frequent gusts in excess of 74 mph with peak gusts of 83 to 94 mph.  Winds in the lower elevations were sustained between 40 and 48 mph with gusts of 63 to 71 mph recorded.  Damage included a downed fence, blown-out window, and a collapsed shed in the area between Riverton and Pavillion.  The wind also toppled an 80-foot tree and dislodged roof shingles in Dubois.  The tree was downed during the afternoon and blocked westbound lanes on Ramshorn Street for several hours.

A strong pressure gradient produced high winds across much of southeastern Wyoming from the evening of February 15th through the afternoon of the 16th.  Gusts from 60 to 70 mph were fairly widespread, with unofficial gusts as high as 77 mph recorded on Interstate 25 south of Wheatland, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gust of 63 mph recorded in Rawlins, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved from the central Rockies into the central Plains, bringing heavy snow to parts of northeastern Wyoming. Snow started during the morning and continued into the night. Six to ten inches of snow were common across southern portions of Campbell and Weston Counties.

A storm system moved from the central Rockies into the central Plains, bringing heavy snow to parts of northeastern Wyoming. Snow started during the morning and continued into the night. Six to ten inches of snow were common across southern portions of Campbell and Weston Counties.

A storm system moved across the northern and central Plains and produced heavy snow across northeastern Wyoming. Four to eight inches of snow fell from the Gillette area, eastward to Moorcroft, the Bear Lodge Mountains, and the Wyoming Black Hills.

A storm system moved across the northern and central Plains and produced heavy snow across northeastern Wyoming. Four to eight inches of snow fell from the Gillette area, eastward to Moorcroft, the Bear Lodge Mountains, and the Wyoming Black Hills.

A strong low pressure system moved across the northern Plains and produced heavy upslope snowfall across the Wyoming Black Hills and the Bear Lodge Mountains. Six to 15 inches of snow fell across much of the area, with the highest amounts around Four Corners. Gusty winds produced blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibilities to a quarter of a mile or less at times.

Strong winds developed during the morning across portions of northeast Wyoming. Winds were strongest across Weston and southern Campbell Counties, where northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph, and gusts to 60 mph, were recorded. Winds diminished during the afternoon.

Gusty southeast winds developed across northeast Wyoming ahead of a strong storm system. Sustained winds of 30 to 45 mph, with gusts to 60 mph, were recorded across most of the area.

An upper level disturbance brought thunderstorms to parts of northeast Wyoming during the late afternoon and evening hours. Gusty winds and hail accompanied a few of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew the roof off a garage. Winds were estimated around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across the region, bringing significant amounts of snowfall to northeastern Wyoming. Eight to 16 inches of snow fell across much of the area, with localized amounts near 24 inches across Campbell County. Strong northwest winds caused considerable blowing and drifting snow and closed many roads, including Interstate 90, for more than 24 hours.
A cold front combined with outflow from convection over Montana produced strong wind gusts of 62 mph at the Cowley and Buffalo airports.

Slow moving low pressure to the south of Wyoming created a prolonged period of upslope snow and rain.  The heaviest precipitation amounts were confined to areas east of the Continental Divide in northern and central Wyoming.  Mountain snowfall amounts generally ranged from 14 to 33 inches with the lower elevations receiving 4 to 8 inches.  Peak snowfall totals occurred in favored upslope areas of the eastern Wind River Mountains, the Bighorn Mountains, and Natrona County.  The 2.20 inches of liquid precipitation received at the Casper airport on Saturday, May 5 was the second highest one-day precipitation total on record for that location.|As the storm moved into the High Plains a tightening pressure gradient created strong northwest wind across northeast Johnson County.  Sustained wind at the Buffalo ASOS frequently topped 40 mph with a peak gust of 66 mph recorded around 6:00 a.m. on Sunday, May 6.

Heavy rain fell across northeastern Wyoming as a strong storm system crossed the region. Two to five inches of rain in 36 hours caused significant runoff and flooding in the Gillette area. The Little Powder River and Donkey Creek rose above bankfull. The Belle Fourche River near Moorcroft crested a half foot below flood stage, but remained within its banks. Some gravel roads were damaged across northern Campbell and western Crook Counties.
Rotating thunderstorms developed in the eastern Foothills of the Bighorn Mountains before moving east across far southern Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported one inch diameter hail in Linch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced very heavy rain and some hail in the Cheyenne vicinity.  Rainfall around 1 inch fell in about 30 minutes over parts of east Cheyenne.
A short-lived tornado touched down over open country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado spotted just east of Interstate 25 near mile marker 94.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced hail south of Cheyenne, Wyoming.
Thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong, compact upper level disturbance.  The storms blossomed in more unstable air east of the Continental Divide and produced damaging wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts on the north side of Lander were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious moisture was brought north into Wyoming in strong monsoonal flow.  Low-level upslope flow from the north aided the development of showers and thunderstorms.  Atmospheric moisture values were around 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall estimated by radar to be three inches or more fell in a swath from Emigrant Gap to Bar Nunn.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding along Poison Spider Road and other nearby roads as culverts could not handle the large volume of water.  Portions of a ranch along Poison Spider Road were under several feet of water.  A mobile home park south of Bar Nunn was flooded as water flowed from surrounding higher terrain.  The lower floor of the rural Poison Spider Elementary School sustained flood damage as the water poured in through several doorways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious moisture was brought north into Wyoming in strong monsoonal flow.  Low-level upslope flow from the north aided the development of showers and thunderstorms.  Atmospheric moisture values were around 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of over one inch fell in about 30 minutes on the west side of Dubois.  Hardest hit were locations between the golf course and U.S. Highway 26.  Culverts were unable to handle the heavy rain, so water flowed over the top of at least one road.  Overflowing washes created temporary waterfalls pushing water and mud across Ramshorn Street (U.S. Highway 26 in Dubois).  A gas station along Warm Springs Drive reported two inches of mud and water inside the associated convenience store.  An antique pool table store had over one-half foot of water inside.  The Dubois cooperative observer recorded 1.39 inches of rainfall during the storm.  This set an all-time daily precipitation record for the station, which has recorded weather observations for nearly 90 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and severe thunderstorms spread south along the eastern slopes of the Bighorn Mountains during the afternoon and early evening.  These storms produced long periods of hail and very heavy rain.  Additional thunderstorms brought heavy rain to areas west and southwest of Casper, including the area near the Jackson Canyon fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Poison Spider Road where water of one foot depth covered the road in four or five places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms within monsoon flow from the southwest again produced heavy rain around the city of Casper.  Rainfall of over one inch in less than 30 minutes was common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of rain fell on already saturated ground west of Casper along Poison Spider Road.  Also, rainfall of 0.50 to 1.50 inches fell in less than one hour in and near the city of Casper.  The rain produced flash flooding along Poison Spider and Paradise Valley roads and in some locales around Paradise Valley.  Urban flooding was also observed along Interstate 25 in Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved off the south end of the Absaroka Range and produced large hail in Hot Springs County.  The Hot Springs County Sheriff's Office reported one inch diameter hail near the intersection of State Highway 120 and Sand Draw Road.
Large hail and heavy rain fell in and just southwest of Jackson.  The brief burst combined to produce flash flooding around town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of around one inch fell in less than one hour causing street flooding east of Town Square in Jackson.  At least one basement was flooded during the storm.  Additionally, pea-size to one-half inch diameter hail occurred for about 15 minutes with the thunderstorm.  The hail covered the ground to a depth of one inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in association with a line of thunderstorms produced two mud and rock slides in Snake River Canyon.  Rainfall was estimated at 0.75 to 1.00 inches in less than 30 minutes.  Both slides occurred between mile markers 118 and 121 on U.S. Highway 89 just east of Alpine.  Vehicles were trapped between the slides for about one hour; however, debris was not completely removed from the road surface for nearly four hours.  The larger of the two slides was five feet deep with rocks and uprooted trees within the debris flow.
Thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong, compact upper level disturbance.  The storms blossomed in more unstable air east of the Continental Divide and produced damaging wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large window was blown-out of a house from thunderstorm wind.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was measured by a Cooperative Observer near Bates Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow produced wind gusts exceeding 70 mph in Lander and the surrounding foothills.  A pole barn and a house that were under construction were damaged by the high wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lander ASOS measured a peak gust to 74 mph at 8:53 p.m. while a gust to 71 mph was recorded three minutes earlier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow produced wind gusts exceeding 70 mph in Lander and the surrounding foothills.  A pole barn and a house that were under construction were damaged by the high wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gables on a pole barn under construction were destroyed on Iiams Road. A home under construction near U.S. Highway 287 and Lyons Valley Road was toppled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious moisture was brought north into Wyoming in strong monsoonal flow.  Low-level upslope flow from the north aided the development of showers and thunderstorms.  Atmospheric moisture values were around 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused three separate mudslides near Valley.  The mud enveloped the Valley School and trapped 15 vehicles on the South Fork Road.  The slides were not entirely cleared for two days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain caused a mud and rock slide across the South Fork Road two miles north of Valley.  The slide was approximately three foot high and covered 50 to 75 feet in length.  Small boulders were included in the debris flow.
A line of thunderstorms moved quickly to the northeast across Yellowstone National Park downing trees in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 12 trees were downed between the South Entrance and Grant Village along the South Entrance Road.  Park Service personnel estimated wind of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain formed northwest of Casper during the late evening.  The storms tracked southeast along and south of U.S. Highway 20/26 over areas that had been hard hit by heavy rain over the previous two weeks.  Natrona County Emergency Management officials reported flooding at the intersection of Poison Spider and 12 Mile roads at 8:57 p.m.  Flooding along Poison Spider Road continued to increase through 10:00 p.m. with additional reports of ditches overflowing in and around Mills.  Heavy rainfall of nearly one inch fell in less than 30 minutes at the Natrona County International Airport causing flooding.  The Poison Spider Elementary School was flooded for the second time in eight days causing upwards of $100,000 in damage.

Monsoon moisture streamed north into Sweetwater County initiating thunderstorms which produced intense rainfall east of Rock Springs.  Rainfall of around one inch fell in less than one hour in a small drainage north of Interstate 80.  Culverts designed to take run-off under the interstate were unable to keep up with the volume of water.  Subsequently, flood waters over-topped the interstate in some spots between mile markers 122 and 125.  A frontage road south of the interstate also experienced flooding as culverts were unable to divert water fast enough.  Traffic was slowed or stopped at times due to water, mud, and debris on the road surface.
Thunderstorms within monsoon flow from the southwest again produced heavy rain around the city of Casper.  Rainfall of over one inch in less than 30 minutes was common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.38 inches in 20 minutes produced flash flooding about three miles north-northeast of downtown Casper.  A private road was washed out, and mud and silt surrounded at least one garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced one inch diameter hail in Jackson.
Outflow from nearby thunderstorms blew apart a 40 foot by 60 foot framed wooden structure under construction.  The wind snapped support poles which were set in concrete.

U.S. Highway 14-16-20 between Cody and Yellowstone National Park's East Entrance was closed due to a mudslide.  The slide was estimated to be three feet deep and 50 to 100 feet in length, which kept the road closed overnight.  The slide occurred near the mouths of Mormon Creek and Eagle Creek near the Eagle Creek Campground.

A strong cold front and upper level system crossed the region, bringing an outbreak of severe weather to Campbell and Crook Counties. Severe thunderstorms developed across central Campbell County early in the afternoon, producing two tornadoes near Gillette and hail to the size of golf balls. These storms weakened as they moved into western Crook County but stregthened again over the northern part of the county.

There were numerous reports of large hail and gusty winds, with brief funnel clouds reported over southern Campbell County.

A line of thunderstorms produced damaging wind across south Big Horn County.  Trees were reported down in Burlington, Greybull, Basin, and along U.S. Highway 14 five miles west of Shell.  The most widespread damage was in Greybull where several metal roofs were ripped from buildings, a store front and awning were torn down, and numerous trees were uprooted.  Also, an historic flagpole in the Greybull town cemetery which had stood since the mid-20th century was significantly bent.

Lightning sparked the Columbine Wildfire over the eastern portion of Yellowstone National Park beginning at 6:00 p.m. on Thursday, August 9.  Smokejumpers and aerial bombardment could not keep the fire contained the next day and the fire grew to over 1,000 acres.  Gusty southwest wind and dry conditions allowed the fire to grow significantly on Sunday, August 12.  The fire made additional advances east over the next three days, twice closing the East Entrance to Yellowstone National Park because of dense smoke.  Over one and one-half inches of rain fell on the fire from August 17 through August 19 considerably slowing progress of the fire.  Several resort areas just to the east of the national park boundary along U.S. Highway 14-16-20 had been threatened by the blaze.  Approximately 18,255 acres were torched.  Total acreage burned within Yellowstone National Park totaled 16,523 with the remainder being in the Shoshone National Forest.

Lightning sparked the Bone Creek Wildfire during the afternoon of Monday, August 13.  The fire began in steep and rugged terrain along and north of Shell Canyon with the main fuels being insect-killed spruce and fir, and lodgepole pine.  By the end of August the wildfire had burned approximately 5,220 acres.  Several forest roads, including Hunt Mountain Road (Forest Road 10), were closed due to the fire.  The fire continued to burn into early September (see September 2007 Storm Data for more information).

Heavy rainfall on the south side of Green River produced a flash flood.  Within 25 minutes, between 1.25 inches and 1.75 inches of rain was measured in this area of town.  A fence at the landfill was washed out by a wall of water six feet high.  The water and mud caused extensive damage to a scale mechanism at the landfill.  Additionally, several basements were flooded, South Hill on State Highway 530 had mud and debris across it, and a pool near one school was completely inundated by mud.  Many yards and streets on the south side of Green River were covered with six to eight inches of mud.

Heavy rainfall in Hoback Canyon caused at least nine mud and rock slides which subsequently closed U.S. Highway 191 around the Sublette-Teton county line.  Crews worked for about seven hours to clear the slides which averaged three to five feet deep.  A guard rail was destroyed by one of the slides.  Marks in a steep ravine indicated flows as deep as 10 feet had swept down nearby hillsides.  Estimated rainfall was 1.00 to 1.50 inches in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bone Creek Wildfire that began on Monday, August 13 continued until Saturday, September 15.  The fire scorched approximately 13,670 acres of forest, almost all of which resided within the borders of Big Horn County.  The passage of a cold front shortly after midnight on Wednesday, September 5 shifted winds to the north allowing the blaze to jump U.S. Highway 14 west of Shell Falls.  Six-tenths of a mile of guardrail burned along the highway which was closed for most of September 5 and 6.  Firefighting costs were estimated at $2.8 million.  No structures were burned in the blaze.  More information about the origins of this wildfire are contained in the August 2007 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious moisture was brought north into Wyoming in strong monsoonal flow.  Low-level upslope flow from the north aided the development of showers and thunderstorms.  Atmospheric moisture values were around 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 1.70 inches of rain in less than 30 minutes in Red Canyon.  The learning center at Red Canyon Ranch was inundated with six inches of mud and water causing extensive damage.  The mud, rock, and water also flooded a barn, shop, and several corrals at the ranch.  Red Canyon Creek and other small tributaries were out of their banks.  Road damage on the ranch was widespread.  The county portion of Red Canyon Road was covered with a three-foot-deep mud flow which closed the road for much of the next week.  At least one nearby residence sustained damage to a basement and destruction of their backyard landscaping due to a mud and water flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across Southern Canada. This combined with high pressure over the Great Basin resulted in high winds along the eastern foothills of the Big Horn Mountains. In addition, a powerful jet stream moved across the Billings Forecast area. The above factors combined with the passage of a strong cold front generated damaging winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Consistent 50-60 mph wind gusts occurred 7 miles west of Sheridan, with a peak wind gust of 61 mph. Wind gusts of 78 mph were reported four miles west/northwest of Dayton.

This combined with high pressure over the Great Basin resulted in high winds along the eastern foothills of the Big Horn Mountains. In addition, a powerful jet stream moved across the Billings Forecast area. The above factors combined with the passage of a strong cold front generated damaging winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down by 70 mph wind gusts in Burgess Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mountain wave produced damaging west wind over the Absaroka Mountains and adjacent Cody Foothills during the overnight hours of November 12 and 13.  Another mountain wave developed along and in the lee of the Bighorn Mountains early on the 13th.  Strong west to southwest wind also occurred across favored locations in central Wyoming in response to a strengthening surface pressure gradient.||Frequent wind gusts of 50 mph or more were recorded at many sites.  Locations in Park, Johnson, Natrona, and Fremont counties observed winds in excess of 75 mph.  About seven miles northeast of Cody the strong wind was able to move an open-sided 50x12x10-foot shed 20 feet from its foundation.  Some notable wind gusts were 91 mph at School House RAWS in the Bighorn Mountains at 5:49 p.m. MST on the 12th and 127 mph on Chief Joseph Pass at 9:33 p.m. MST on the 12th.||In addition, more than 75 firefighters battled a wildfire near Clark where wind speeds were estimated to be gusting over 75 mph.  The fire stretched for about two miles along Line Creek.  One firefighter said rocks up to the size of golf balls were being thrown by the wind even shattering the back window of a fire engine.  One cabin was destroyed, two outbuildings were consumed by flames, and one car and a horse trailer were lost.  Many homes were evacuated during the blaze which started about 7:00 p.m. Monday the 12th and was not completely extinguished until around noon on Tuesday.

Heavy rain fell across northeastern Wyoming as a strong storm system crossed the region. Two to five inches of rain in 36 hours caused significant runoff and flooding in the Gillette area. The Little Powder River and Donkey Creek rose above bankfull. The Belle Fourche River near Moorcroft crested a half foot below flood stage, but remained within its banks. Some gravel roads were damaged across northern Campbell and western Crook Counties.

A lightning caused wildfire burned 4,827 acres 15 miles southwest of Sheridan. The fire burned from approximately August 10th to August 21st and consisted mostly of Lodgepole Pine.

A large spring storm associated with a cutoff upper level low, moved across the Northern Rockies on the afternoon of June 6th through the morning of June7th. Thunderstorms developed by early afternoon across northern Wyoming and southern Montana, with heavy rainfall beginning in Sheridan, Wyoming and Carbon County, Montana at 330pm and across much of southern Montana by 6pm. By 9pm Wednesday evening, heavy rainfall became more widespread across southern Montana and continued thruogh the overnight hours. By mid morning on the 6th, rainfall had ended across the impacted area. Runoff from areas upstream of Rosebud County and the Tongue River Reservoir resulted in flooding downstream of the dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A park was closed in Ranchester due to standing water. Three inches of rain was reported in the Sheridan gage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds blew across open areas close to the southeast Wyoming mountains  and southern Platte county.  Six tractor-trailer trucks were reported blown over, mostly on Interstate 80 in Carbon county.  A few houses were damaged in far southern Converse county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many gusts around 70 mph on Interstate 25 at the Bordeaux interchange, with a peak gust of 86 mph recorded at 03:56 A.M. MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mountain wave wind event developed on the east side of the Wind River Mountain range causing strong mountaintop winds to descend the east slopes and into the adjacent Lander Foothills.  Gusts over 50 mph lasted for over three hours and a peak gust of 74 mph was measured at Hunt Field at 1:37 p.m. MDT.  The strong wind downed several trees, damaged house roofs around Lander, and fanned a grass fire in the Baldwin Creek drainage.  The grass fire engulfed one small barn as it made a one mile run to the east.

A severe thunderstorm rapidly developed just west of Sheridan and moved over the city during the early evening hours of the 7th. Numerous street flooding along with downed power lines and tree limbs were reported.
A severe thunderstorm produced hail to half dollar size over southeastern Weston County.
A couple of severe thunderstorms produced hail over parts of central Laramie county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter at the Terry Bison Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed in strong southerly flow ahead of an approaching low pressure system caused damaging winds across portions of southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust at Big Piney Airport.
